frameworks.name: resource-rational planning
frameworks.one_line: non-instrumental information is valuable when planning benefits exceed cognitive and financial costs

frameworks.name: intolerance of uncertainty
frameworks.one_line: trait anxiety construct hypothesized to drive information-seeking, here tested empirically

core_question: Does trait anxiety modulate willingness to pay for non-instrumental information, and through what cognitive mechanism?
key_assumptions: non-instrumental information is useful for planning ahead even when outcome cannot be changed
rational agents pay for information only when anticipated planning benefits exceed costs
trait anxiety dimensions are dissociable (somatic vs cognitive anxiety, negative affect, self-esteem)
manipulations: cost to know whether the outcome will happen
trait anxiety factor scores across individuals
key_findings: high trait somatic anxiety predicts stronger preference for non-instrumental information
effect is specific to somatic anxiety — not cognitive anxiety, negative affect, or low self-esteem
preference holds across outcome valence, probability, and information cost
resource-rational model fit attributes the effect to decreased subjective cost of information
interpretations: findings support the intolerance-of-uncertainty hypothesis of anxiety
trait somatic anxiety specifically modulates the desire for information via subjective information-cost
